title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: sumvar package provides simple easy use tools summarising continuous categorical data, inspired Stata’s “sum” “tab” commands. functions tidyverse/dplyr pipe-friendly return tibbles.

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: Simple one-line commands explore variables R users Pipe-friendly tidyverse integration Tabular summaries can stored tibbles used downstream analysis. first moved Stata R 5 years ago, main thing missed simplicity “sum” “tab” functions efficiently explore data. template code perform commands, introductory R books tutorials eg. https://r4ds.hadley.nz/data-tidy.html, takes typically 3-5 lines replicate functions R. couldn’t find package quite simply efficiently explore data. Sumvar fast easy use, brings variable summary functions R.

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: call dist_sum() explore continous variable. tibble output shows: number rows data, number missing, median, interquartile range (25th 75th centiles), mean, standard deviation, 95% confidence intervals using Wald method (normal approximation), minimum maximum values. Dist_sum() show density plot histogram single variable, grouped density plot grouping varialbe. can save output dist_sum tibble use estimates downstream analysis, eg. sum_df <- df %>% dist_sum(age, sex)
code: # Example data set.seed(123) df <- tibble::tibble(   age = rnorm(100, mean = 50, sd = 20),   sex = sample(c("male", "female"), 100, replace = TRUE)) %>%   dplyr::mutate(age = dplyr::if_else(sex == "male", age + 10, age))  # Call dist_sum df %>% dist_sum(age) #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value with `binwidth`. #> # A tibble: 1 × 11 #>       n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd ci_lower ci_upper   min   max #>   <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1   100      0   55.6  44.0  68.1  56.9  18.2     53.3     60.5  13.8  101. df %>% dist_sum(age, sex) #> # A tibble: 2 × 14 #>   sex        n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max ci_lower #>   <chr>  <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 female    49      0   52.5  41.1  65.6  54.7  17.6  16.3  93.7     49.8 #> 2 male      51      0   57.2  46.8  71.3  59.0  18.6  13.8 101.      53.9 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ci_upper <dbl>, p_ttest <dbl>, p_wilcox <dbl>

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: explore distribution dates, call dist_date() - similar dist_sum. can also grouped second grouping variable. single date, histogram shown; grouping variable also called, density plot shown.
code: df3 <- tibble::tibble(   dates = as.Date("2022-01-01") + rnorm(n=100, sd=50, mean=0),   group = sample(c("A", "B"), 100, TRUE)) %>%   dplyr::mutate(dt = dplyr::case_when(group == "A" ~ dates + 10, TRUE ~ dates))  df3 %>% dist_date(dates) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1   100      0 2021-10-25 2021-11-26 2021-12-22 2022-01-28 2022-06-12 df3 %>% dist_date(dates, group) #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>   group     n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <chr> <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1 A        43      0 2021-10-25 2021-11-25 2021-12-17 2022-01-16 2022-06-12 #> 2 B        57      0 2021-10-27 2021-12-01 2022-01-03 2022-02-07 2022-04-20

title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: tab1() produces tibble showing distribution categorical variable illustrates using horizontal bar chart.
code: df2 <- tibble::tibble(   group = sample(LETTERS[1:3], 200, TRUE) )  df2 %>% tab1(group)

what: Check for duplicate and missing data
title: Exploring data with sumvar
text: explore proportion duplicate values missing values variable, pass dup().  send whole database dup(), produce summary duplicates missingness whole database. Dup() illustrates stacked bar chart.
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 20 values with missing.  example_data %>% dup(age) #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   Variable     n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>    <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age        200      119          66              35.7        15 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl> example_data <- dplyr::tibble(age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0),                               sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 200, TRUE),                               favourite_colour = sample(c("Red", "Blue", "Purple"), 200, TRUE)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 32)] <- NA  # Replace 32 values with missing.  dup(example_data) #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   Variable             n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>            <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age                200      117          68              36.8        15 #> 2 sex                200        2         166              98.8        32 #> 3 favourite_colour   200        3         197              98.5         0 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl>

title: Authors and Citation
text: Alexander Stockdale. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Stockdale (2025). sumvar: Summarise Continuous, Date Categorical Variables, Check Duplicates Missing Data. R package version 0.2, https://github.com/alstockdale/sumvar.
code: @Manual{,   title = {sumvar: Summarise Continuous, Date and Categorical Variables, Check for Duplicates and Missing Data},   author = {Alexander Stockdale},   year = {2025},   note = {R package version 0.2},   url = {https://github.com/alstockdale/sumvar}, }

what: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: Summarises minimum, maximum, median, interquartile range date variable, optionally stratified grouping variable. Produces histogram (optionally) density plot.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: data data frame tibble. var date variable summarise. Optional grouping variable.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
text: tibble summary statistics date variable.

title: Summarize and visualize a date variable — dist_date
code: # Example ungrouped df <- tibble::tibble(   dt = as.Date("2020-01-01") + sample(0:1000, 100, TRUE) ) dist_date(df, dt)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>       n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1   100      0 2020-01-12 2020-09-20 2021-07-06 2022-03-06 2022-09-24  # Example grouped df2 <- tibble::tibble(   dt = as.Date("2020-01-01") + sample(0:1000, 100, TRUE),   grp = sample(1:2, 100, TRUE) ) dist_date(df2, dt, grp)  #> # A tibble: 2 × 8 #>     grp     n n_miss min        p25        median     p75        max        #>   <int> <int>  <int> <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     <date>     #> 1     1    48      0 2020-01-13 2020-09-25 2021-04-29 2022-02-11 2022-09-24 #> 2     2    52      0 2020-01-04 2020-10-21 2021-06-02 2022-01-27 2022-08-12 # Note this function accepts a pipe from dplyr eg. df %>% dist_date(date_var, group_var)

title: Explore a continuous variable. — dist_sum
text: Summarises median, interquartile range, mean, standard deviation, confidence intervals mean produces density plot, stratified second grouping variable. Provides frequentist hypothesis tests comparison groups: T test Wilcoxon rank sum 2 groups, Anova Kruskall wallis test 3 groups. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble.

title: Explore a continuous variable. — dist_sum
text: data data frame tibble var variable like summarise grouping variable

title: Explore a continuous variable. — dist_sum
text: tibble summary variable frequency (n), number missing observations (n_miss), median, interquartile range, mean, SD, 95% confidence intervals mean (using Z distribution), density plots. Shows T test (p_ttest) Wilcoxon rank sum (p_wilcox) hypothesis tests two groups Anova test (p_anova) Kruskal-Wallis test (p_kruskal) three groups.

title: Explore a continuous variable. — dist_sum
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                               group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                               size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, group)  #> # A tibble: 4 × 14 #>   group     n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max ci_lower #>   <chr> <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 a        33      0   32.6  23.6  39.6  31.4 10.6  12.1   55.6     27.8 #> 2 b        25      0   29.2  23.7  37.1  29.3  8.90  9.36  43.2     25.9 #> 3 c        20      0   33.8  23.7  38.4  31.7 11.9   5.53  56.5     26.4 #> 4 d        22      0   26.9  20.3  36.4  28.8 11.9  11.0   52.1     23.8 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ci_upper <dbl>, p_anova <dbl>, p_kruskal <dbl> example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, age = rnorm(100, mean = 30, sd = 10),                              sex = sample(c("male", "female"),                              size = 100, replace = TRUE)) dist_sum(example_data, age, sex)  #> # A tibble: 2 × 14 #>   sex        n n_miss median   p25   p75  mean    sd   min   max ci_lower #>   <chr>  <int>  <int>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 female    54      0   29.0  24.5  35.9  30.3  9.30  10.2  51.3     27.8 #> 2 male      46      0   34.0  25.9  40.9  33.4 10.9   11.8  50.9     30.3 #> # ℹ 3 more variables: ci_upper <dbl>, p_ttest <dbl>, p_wilcox <dbl> summary <- dist_sum(example_data, age, sex) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.

what: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: Provides integer value number duplicates found within variable function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% dup(variable)

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: data data frame tibble var variable assess

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
text: tibble number percentage duplicate values found, number missing values (NA), together percentages.

title: Explore duplicate and missing data — dup
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:200, age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. dup(example_data, age)  #> # A tibble: 1 × 7 #>   Variable     n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>    <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age        200      116          69              37.3        15 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl> # It is also possible to pass a whole database to dup and it will explore all variables. example_data <- dplyr::tibble(age = round(rnorm(200, mean = 30, sd = 50), digits=0),                               sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 200, TRUE),                               favourite_colour = sample(c("Red", "Blue", "Purple"), 200, TRUE)) example_data$age[sample(1:200, size = 15)] <- NA  # Replace 15 values with missing. example_data$sex[sample(1:200, size = 32)] <- NA  # Replace 32 values with missing. dup(example_data)  #> # A tibble: 3 × 7 #>   Variable             n n_unique n_duplicate percent_duplicate n_missing #>   <chr>            <int>    <int>       <int>             <dbl>     <int> #> 1 age                200      115          70              37.8        15 #> 2 sex                200        2         166              98.8        32 #> 3 favourite_colour   200        3         197              98.5         0 #> # ℹ 1 more variable: percent_missing <dbl>

what: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: Analyses data frame tibble, summarising continuous, date categorical variables, missing data duplicate values, produces HTML PDF report.

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
code: explorer(   data,   output_file = NULL,   format = c("html", "pdf"),   progress = TRUE,   id_var = NULL )

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: data data frame tibble explore. output_file name output file. Default uses <df_name>_report.html .pdf format Output format, either "html" (default) "pdf". progress TRUE (default), show progress bar building report. id_var Character vector column names treat IDs (summarised).

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
text: Outputs html PDF summary. Output PDF typically takes longer. PDF output, LaTeX distribution must installed. TinyTeX recommended. install, run:

title: Explore all variables and generate an HTML or PDF summary report — explorer
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Build example data from mtcars with some factors and a date column:   cars_example <- mtcars %>%     dplyr::mutate(       across(c(vs, am, gear, carb, cyl), as.factor),       date_var = as.Date("2025-06-01") +         sample(-300:300, nrow(mtcars), replace = TRUE),       id = dplyr::row_number()     ) # To run explorer:   explorer(mtcars)                  # with progress bar   explorer(mtcars, progress = FALSE) # omit progress bar   explorer(mtcars, format = "pdf")   # PDF output   explorer(mtcars, format = "pdf", id_var = "id")   # Identify ID variable } # }

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: See magrittr::%>% details.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: lhs value magrittr placeholder. rhs function call using magrittr semantics.

title: Pipe operator — %>%
text: result calling rhs(lhs).

what: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables in R — sumvar
title: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables in R — sumvar
text: sumvar package explores continuous categorical variables. sumvar brings ease simplicity "sum" "tab" functions Stata R. explore continuous variable, use dist_sum(). can stratify grouping variable: df %>% dist_sum(var, group) explore dates, use dist_date(); usage dist_sum(). summarise single categorical variable use tab1(), e.g. df %>% tab1(var). two-way table, use tab(), e.g. df %>% tab(var1, var2). include options frequentist hypothesis tests. Explore duplicates missing values dup(). functions tidyverse/dplyr-friendly accept %>% pipe, outputting results tibble. can save outputs manipulation, e.g. summary <- df %>% dist_sum(var).

title: sumvar: Summarise Continuous and Categorical Variables in R — sumvar
text: Maintainer: Alexander Stockdale .stockdale@liverpool.ac.uk

what: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: Creates "n x n" cross-tabulation two categorical variables, row percentages. Includes options adding frequentist hypothesis testing. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% tab(variable1, variable2)

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: data data frame tibble variable1 first categorical variable variable2 second categorical variable test Optional frequentist hypothesis test, use test=exact Fisher's exact test=chi Chi squared

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
text: tibble cross-tabulation frequencies row percentages

title: Create a cross-tabulation of two categorial variables — tab
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, group1 = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE),                                                   group2= sample(c("male", "female"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE)) example_data$group1[sample(1:100, size = 10)] <- NA  # Replace 10 with missing tab(example_data, group1, group2) #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   group1 N_female N_male Percent_female Percent_male #>   <chr>     <int>  <int>          <dbl>        <dbl> #> 1 a             9     11           45           55   #> 2 b             7     15           31.8         68.2 #> 3 c            15     16           48.4         51.6 #> 4 d            13      4           76.5         23.5 #> 5 NA            5      5           50           50   summary <- tab(example_data, group1, group2) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: Summarises frequencies percentages categorical variable. function accepts input dplyr pipe "%>%" outputs results tibble. eg. example_data %>% tab1(variable)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: data data frame tibble variable categorical variable like summarise dp number decimal places percentages (default=2)

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
text: tibble frequencies percentages

title: Summarise a categorial variable — tab1
code: example_data <- dplyr::tibble(id = 1:100, group = sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d"),                                                   size = 100, replace = TRUE)) example_data$group[sample(1:100, size = 10)] <- NA  # Replace 10 with missing tab1(example_data, group)  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               26 26.0    #> 2 c               26 26.0    #> 3 b               20 20.0    #> 4 d               18 18.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0   summary <- tab1(example_data, group) # Save summary statistics as a tibble.  #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   Category Frequency Percent #>   <chr>        <int> <chr>   #> 1 a               26 26.0    #> 2 c               26 26.0    #> 3 b               20 20.0    #> 4 d               18 18.0    #> 5 NA              10 10.0    #> 6 Total          100 100.0
